topic: Decision Making to Improve Human Resource Performance
subject: business-studies

items.q: Does a high labour turnover always indicate poor management?
items.correct: Not necessarily — some turnover brings fresh ideas and skills, and rates vary by industry; but persistently high turnover raises recruitment and training costs.
items.misconception: Any labour turnover is bad and should be reduced to zero.
items.random_wrong: Labour turnover measures how quickly stock is sold.
items.why: Students treat turnover as purely negative; a moderate level can be healthy, and zero turnover can signal stagnation.

items.q: Is paying workers more the best way to motivate them?
items.correct: Not always — Herzberg argued pay mainly prevents dissatisfaction as a hygiene factor, while motivation comes from the work itself, recognition and responsibility.
items.misconception: Higher pay always produces proportionally higher motivation and productivity.
items.random_wrong: Pay is illegal to use as a motivator under UK law.
items.why: Students default to money as the universal motivator, overlooking non-financial methods like job enrichment and empowerment.

items.q: Does delayering always improve HR performance?
items.correct: No — it cuts costs and speeds communication, but it widens spans of control, can overload remaining managers, and may damage morale and promotion prospects.
items.misconception: Removing management layers always makes a business more efficient.
items.random_wrong: Delayering means redesigning the office floor layout.
items.why: Students see delayering only as a cost saving, missing the trade-off with workload, morale and control.

items.q: A firm with 200 staff has 20 leavers this year. Its labour turnover is:
items.why: Correct answer: 10%. “20%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is a measure of labour productivity?
items.correct: Output per employee per time period
items.misconception: Total revenue divided by total costs
items.random_wrong: Leavers divided by average staff employed
items.why: It's Output per employee per time period. “Total revenue divided by total costs” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A wider span of control means:
items.correct: Each manager is directly responsible for more subordinates
items.misconception: The business has more levels of hierarchy
items.why: Correct answer: Each manager is directly responsible for more subordinates. “The business has more levels of hierarchy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Removing levels of management from the hierarchy
items.why: It's Removing levels of management from the hierarchy. “Recruiting additional supervisors” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A business produces 12,000 units per month with 60 employees. What is labour productivity in units per employee?
items.why: Exact answer: 200 units per employee. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
